sia.note: A quick walkthrough of creating a voting, inviting participants and verifying results on blockchain

q1.a: VoteMe is a blockchain-oriented electronic voting platform designed for corporate, educational, and civic contexts. It records vote commitments and result hashes on the Ethereum Sepolia testnet, so anyone can independently verify that results have not been tampered with.

q2.a: VoteMe serves four roles: organizers who create and manage elections, voters who cast votes in elections they are invited to, auditors who have read-only access to results and audit logs, and global administrators who manage platform settings. Unauthenticated users can still verify results through the public audit log.

q3.q: How do I create an account?
q3.a: Click "Get started" on the home page and fill in your name, email, and password. After submitting the registration form you will receive a confirmation email — follow the link in that email to activate your account before logging in.

q1.q: How do I join an election?
q1.a: Organizers can invite you by a direct link, an invitation code, or a QR code. Paste the invitation code on the Join page, scan the QR code, or follow the link you received — all three methods take you directly to the election ballot.

q2.q: How do I cast a vote?
q2.a: Open the election ballot, select your preferred option, and proceed to the preview step where you can review your choice before confirming. After you confirm, your vote is submitted and recorded. The flow has three steps on purpose: select, preview, confirm — there is no way to rush past the preview.

q3.q: Can I change my vote after submitting?
q3.a: No. Once a vote is submitted it is final and cannot be changed or withdrawn. This is enforced both in the database (a unique constraint per voter per election) and at the smart contract level on the blockchain.

q4.q: Can I vote more than once in the same election?
q4.a: No. The system enforces a hard one-vote-per-person-per-election rule. If you attempt to vote again the backend will reject the request with a conflict error, and the UI will show a "You have already voted" message.

q1.q: How do I create an election?
q1.a: Log in with an organizer account, navigate to Elections, and click "Create Voting". Fill in the title, description, start and end date/time, access type (public, whitelist, or invitation), and add the voting options. Once saved the election is in draft status and you can edit it before publishing.

q2.a: From the election management page you can generate an invitation link, a shareable code, or a QR code. You can also upload a CSV file to define a voter whitelist, which restricts participation to only the listed email addresses.

q3.a: Elections end automatically when the scheduled end date and time is reached — a background scheduler handles this. Once finished, the result hash is recorded on the blockchain and the results become visible to authorized viewers. Organizers cannot extend the end time after the election has been published.

q1.a: Results are tallied automatically when the election finishes. Each option receives a vote count and percentage breakdown. The final result data is hashed and the hash is written to the Ethereum Sepolia smart contract, creating an immutable on-chain record.

q2.q: How do I verify results on the blockchain?
q2.a: On the results page you will see a transaction hash for the on-chain result record. Click it to open the transaction on Etherscan Sepolia (sepolia.etherscan.io), where you can inspect the contract call and confirm the result hash independently — no VoteMe account required.

q3.a: The audit log is an immutable, append-only record of every significant action on the platform: election creation, status changes, vote submissions, and result tallying. Auditors and administrators can browse the log to reconstruct the full history of any election.

q1.a: Votes are linked to your user account internally for duplicate-vote prevention, but the results dashboard shows only aggregate counts — not which person voted for which option. Personal data is never stored on the blockchain; only cryptographic hashes and event proofs go on-chain.

q2.a: When an election is published, a hash of its parameters is written to the smart contract on Ethereum Sepolia. When it finishes, a hash of the final results is written as well. Because blockchain records are immutable, any third party can verify that neither the election rules nor the results were altered after the fact.

q3.a: Only hashes and event proofs — never personal data. Specifically: a hash of election parameters at publication time and a hash of result data at finalization time. All personally identifiable information (names, emails, vote choices) stays in the private PostgreSQL database and is never published to the blockchain.
